steps.title: Identify what needs to be joined
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses separated by the blank: “Okinaka doesn’t make such decisions single-handedly” and “[all] historical designations must be approved by a group of nine other experts.” The word however is a conjunctive adverb, not a coordinating conjunction. A conjunctive adverb cannot join two independent clauses with only a comma — that creates a comma splice. It needs either a semicolon before it and a comma after (IC; however, IC) or a period before it, starting a new sentence (IC. However, IC).

steps.title: Eliminate comma-splice options and confirm the correct pattern
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘however, all’: Puts only a comma before however — a classic comma splice between two independent clauses. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘however and all’: Adds and after however, making the conjunction redundant and grammatically incoherent. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘however all’: No punctuation before however at all — a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘however. All’: Ends the first independent clause with a period after the comma-following-however position, then begins a new sentence with a capital “A” — the valid IC. However, IC pattern (with the period absorbed into the period after the first clause). ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
